items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Paul Kennedy on Great Powers
items.textEn: What a profound honor to have Paul Kennedy on the ChinaTalk podcast. Kennedy is my favorite living historian and the writer who’s most shaped my intellectual development. His analysis underpins what you hear on this show every week. The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers is an epochal work that traces global power transitions from 1500 to the present. It’s gripping, forest-and-trees scholarship at its finest. Equally impressive in different ways is his book, The Rise of Anglo-German Antagonism, 1860 to 1914 . Not only is it god-tier diplomatic history, but it also gives you a feel for the era through its explorations of social, economic, domestic, political, and cultural dimensions of Anglo-German relations. There are fascinating US/China analogies that we’ll get into in this podcast. His two most recent works directly inform the military coverage on ChinaTalk. Engineers of Victory looks at how people and the systems they worked within solved engineering challenges that turned the tide for entire theaters in World War II. His latest, Victory at Sea: Naval Power and the Transformation of Global Order in World War II , is a sweeping history of a radical transformation in the balance of military power, from the mid-1930s when America was just gaining prominence, to after World War II, when it had no other significant naval competitor. The Parliament of Man: A History of the United Nations made me interested in international organizations and gave me my senior thesis topic about the creation of the UN. What Kennedy taught me more than anything is this — sweat the details, look at the individual players, and zoom out often enough to understand what truly shapes the long-term fate of nations. Over the course of this episode, we pick up themes from all across his work: Great Power rivalries of the late 19th-early 20th centuries, the China echoes today, and why potential antagonisms turn nice and others turn belligerent, The persistent struggles of liberal internationalists and why they rarely get the outcomes they want, Why China today is not Germany of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, The surprising ways geography shapes global power dynamics, How fear spreads among nations and why mutual suspicion is so hard to escape, Why top powers blow it and lose their dominant place in the world, How systems and innovation win wars. And much more, including salutary lessons from the Dutch and Swedes on boring yet prosperous futures, how Churchill’s interest in gadgets influenced the course of the Second World War, and why transformative action from the UN remains unlikely in the near future. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . How to Win Friends and Alienate Nations Jordan Schneider: What is national power, and why does it matter? Paul Kennedy: Since the earliest struggles between tribes and later between larger groups of people with their symbols and more organized armies, and in particular, the emergence of the modern nation-state around the year 1500 (as historians roughly date it) — this entity has existed. The nation-state, with its capacity to raise taxes, conscript, and enroll men to fight under the direction of, say, a Dutch republic or a Spanish monarchy, has fundamentally shaped global affairs. Since that era, with the rise of the nation-state in Western Europe and its subsequent adoption worldwide, this thing we call "the country," "the nation," or "the state," has operated within what political scientists term an anarchic international system. "Anarchy" here doesn’t necessarily mean everyone slaughtering everyone else, but simply the absence of a higher authority controlling global affairs. The United Nations General Assembly lists approximately 189 or 192 nation-states, large and small. We give them all a membership, a vote in the General Assembly. Almost all of them have some sort of diplomatic service as well as a domestic home service, have a government and a constitution of one form or another, and inte…
items.summaryZh: 文章介绍了历史学家保罗·肯尼迪在ChinaTalk播客的访谈，概述了其主要著作及其对理解大国兴衰、国际秩序和中美关系的重要性，提醒投资者关注历史视角下的地缘政治走势。
items.translationZh:
标题：保罗·肯尼迪论大国

正文：能在ChinaTalk播客上邀请到保罗·肯尼迪，实在是莫大的荣幸。肯尼迪是我最喜爱的在世历史学家，也是对我的智识发展影响最大的作家。他的分析支撑了本节目每周的内容。《大国的兴衰》（The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers）是一部划时代的著作，追溯了从1500年至今的全球权力转移。它引人入胜，是宏观与微观研究的极致。另一部以不同方式同样令人印象深刻的作品是《英德敌意的崛起，1860‑1914》（The Rise of Anglo‑German Antagonism, 1860 to 1914）。这不仅是顶尖的外交史，更通过对英德关系的社会、经济、国内、政治和文化层面的探讨，让人切身感受到那个时代。本次播客将讨论其中的美中类比。

他的两部最新作品直接为ChinaTalk的军事报道提供了依据。《胜利的工程师》（Engineers of Victory）研究了二战期间人们及其所处体系如何解决工程难题，从而扭转整个战区的局势。他最新的《海上胜利：海军力量与二战后全球秩序的转变》（Victory at Sea: Naval Power and the Transformation of Global Order in World War II），是一部宏观叙事，阐述了自1930年代中期美国刚崭露头角，到二战结束后已无其他重要海军竞争者的军事力量平衡的根本性转变。《人类议会》（The Parliament of Man: A History of the United Nations）让我对国际组织产生兴趣，也成为我关于联合国创建的毕业论文题目。肯尼迪教会我最重要的一点是——关注细节，观察个体行动者，并且经常抽离全局，以把握真正决定国家长期命运的因素。

在本期节目中，我们将从他的全部著作中提炼主题：19世纪末至20世纪初大国竞争、当代中国的呼应、为何某些潜在冲突会转为友好、而另一些则变得敌对；自由国际主义者的持续奋斗以及为何他们的目标鲜有实现；为何当代中国不同于19世纪末至20世纪初的德国；地理如何出人意料地塑造全球权力动态；恐惧如何在国家间蔓延，以及为何相互怀疑难以摆脱；为何霸主会失误并失去其支配地位；系统与创新如何决定战争胜负。还有更多内容，包括荷兰和瑞典在平凡却繁荣的未来中的启示、丘吉尔对小发明的兴趣如何影响二战进程，以及为何联合国在近期内实现根本性变革的可能性仍然渺茫。立即在您喜爱的播客应用中收听。

如何赢得友谊并疏远国家——乔丹·施耐德：什么是国家力量，为什么重要？保罗·肯尼迪：自最早的部落争斗以及后来的更大群体之间的冲突——伴随符号和更有组织的军队，尤其是大约在1500年左右出现的现代民族国家（历史学家大致如此界定）——这种实体一直存在。民族国家具备征税、征兵并让人民在如荷兰共和国或西班牙君主制的指挥下作战的能力，这根本塑造了全球事务。从那时起，随着民族国家在西欧的崛起并随后被全球所采纳，我们所谓的“国家”“民族”或“政体”，在政治科学家所称的无政府国际体系（anarchic international system）中运作。这里的“无政府”（anarchy）并不一定意味着人人相残，而是指缺乏一个更高层次的权威来控制全球事务。联合国大会列出了大约189或192个民族国家，无论大小。我们为它们全部提供会员资格、在大会中的投票权。几乎所有国家都有某种外交机构和国内服务机构，拥有政府和某种形式的宪法，等等。
